conditions","user":{"login":"pogudingleb","id":24942839,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0OTQyODM5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/24942839?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb","html_url":"https://github.com/pogudingleb","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-11-22T20:42:24Z","updated_at":"2023-11-22T21:42:09Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"COLLABORATOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/pulls/249","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/pull/249","diff_url":"https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/pull/249.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/pull/249.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR adds two functions `assess_identifiability_kic` and `identifiable_functions_kic` which take as input, in addition to an ode also a list of functions, for which initial conditions are assumed to be known. In this context, the functions assess identifiability or find all identifiable functions in parameters and initial conditions, respectively.

For example:
```julia
using StructuralIdentifiability: assess_identifiability_kic

ode = @ODEmodel(
    x1'(t) = a * x1(t) - b * x1(t) * x2(t),
    x2'(t) = -c * x2(t) + d * x1(t) * x2(t),
    y(t) = x1(t)
)
```


Algorithmically, first identifiable functions are computed (`with_states=true`) and then the known initial conditions are added to them as generator of a fields following by an additional simplification step. PR adds two functions `assess_identifiability_kic` and `identifiable_functions_kic` which take as input, in addition to an ode also a list of functions, for which initial conditions are assumed to be known. In this context, the functions assess identifiability or find all identifiable functions in parameters and initial conditions, respectively.

For example:
```julia
using StructuralIdentifiability: assess_identifiability_kic

ode = @ODEmodel(
    x1'(t) = a * x1(t) - b * x1(t) * x2(t),
    x2'(t) = -c * x2(t) + d * x1(t) * x2(t),
    y(t) = x1(t)
)

assess_identifiability_kic(ode, [x2])
```
will return
```
OrderedDict{Nemo.fmpq_mpoly, Symbol} with 6 entries:
  x1(0) => :globally
  x2(0) => :globally
  a     => :globally
  b     => :globally
  c     => :globally
  d     => :globally
```

Algorithmically, first identifiable functions are computed (`with_states=true`) and then the known initial conditions are added to them as generator of a fields following by an additional simplification step. The computation can be done more efficiently if the generators are added earlier, but this would complicate code quite a bit, not so clear if this is a good idea right now.

What is missing is the version of this functionality for local conditions","user":{"login":"pogudingleb","id":24942839,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI0OTQyODM5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/24942839?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb","html_url":"https://github.com/pogudingleb","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pogudingleb/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This PR adds two functions `assess_identifiability_kic` and `identifiable_functions_kic` which take as input, in addition to an ode also a list of functions, for which initial conditions are assumed to be known. In this context, the functions assess identifiability or find all identifiable functions in parameters and initial conditions, respectively.

For example:
```julia
using StructuralIdentifiability: assess_identifiability_kic

ode = @ODEmodel(
    x1'(t) = a * x1(t) - b * x1(t) * x2(t),
    x2'(t) = -c * x2(t) + d * x1(t) * x2(t),
    y(t) = x1(t)
)

assess_identifiability_kic(ode, [x2])
```
will return
```
OrderedDict{Nemo.fmpq_mpoly, Symbol} with 6 entries:
  x1(0) => :globally
  x2(0) => :globally
  a     => :globally
  b     => :globally
  c     => :globally
  d     => :globally
```

Algorithmically, first identifiable functions are computed (`with_states=true`) and then the known initial conditions are added to them as generator of a fields following by an additional simplification step. The computation can be done more efficiently if the generators are added earlier, but this would complicate code quite a bit, not so clear if this is a good idea right now.

What is missing is the version of this functionality for local As a general outline, three pieces of code are needed: the GB backend in [Groebner.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl), the evaluation-interpolation scheme in [ParamPunPam.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/ParamPunPam.jl), and the frontend in [StructuralIdentifiability.jl](https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl). StructuralIdentifiability.jl will use ParamPunPam.jl to compute Groebner bases with parameters, which, in turn, will use Groebner.jl as a building block. Both ParamPunPam.jl and Groebner.jl are already usable for this purpose to some extent. 

I think a good goal for the very nearest future is to have a working prototype in SI.jl and to look at the challenging examples more closely to decide what needs to be optimized. I suggest the first milestones:

- [ ] Implementing a simple interface in StructuralIdentifiability.jl #187
- [x] Finishing the prep work in Groebner.jl, https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl/pull/67
- [x] Implementing tracing in Groebner.jl

-- ```juila
```juila
ERROR: Unsatisfiable requirements detected for package MultivariatePolynomials [102ac46a]:
 MultivariatePolynomials [102ac46a] log:
 ├─possible versions are: 0.2.0-0.5.2 or uninstalled
:
:
 └─restricted by compatibility requirements with Groebner [0b43b601] to versions: 0.4.3-0.4.7 — no versions left
   └─Groebner [0b43b601] log:
     ├─possible versions are: 0.1.0-0.4.4 or uninstalled
     ├─restricted by compatibility requirements with StructuralIdentifiability [220ca800] to versions: 0.2.0-0.3.6
     │ └─StructuralIdentifiability [220ca800] log:
     │   ├─possible versions are: 0.1.0-0.4.10 or uninstalled
     │   └─restricted to versions 0.4 by an explicit requirement, leaving only versions: 0.4.0-0.4.10
     └─restricted by compatibility requirements with Symbolics [0c5d862f] to versions: 0.3.0-0.4.4, leaving only versions: 0.3.0-0.3.6
       └─Symbolics [0c5d862f] log: see above
```

am trying to write an extension for [Catalyst](https://github.com/SciML/Catalyst.jl)/StructuralIdentifiability that would allow user to input by Catalyst defined `ReactionSystem`s to `assess_identifiability` and `assess_local_identifiability`. 

Basically, `ReactionSystem`s can be converted to `ODESystems`, so I would simply use this conversion. However, generally, Catalyst does not have observables directly defined, so I wanted the user to input these using measured_quantities. Typically, they would just be species of the system, so the user would do something like
```julia StructuralIdentifiability
rs = @reaction_network begin
    (p,d), 0 <--> X
    (k1,k2), X <--> Y
end
assess_identifiability(rs; measured_quantities=[:Y])
```
and the appropriate conversion would be done under the hood. Also, we could do some automatic removal of conserved quantities to e.g. deal with cases like: https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/189

However, I was trying to prototype things following something like: https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/133 but fails. Here is my minimal example:
```julia
# Fectch packages.
using Catalyst, StructuralIdentifiability

# Create model and convert to ODESystem.
rn = @reaction_network begin
    (k1,k2), X <--> Y
end
osys = convert(ODESystem,rn)

# Create equation for observables (X only).
@variables t y(t)
eq = y~states(osys)[1]

# Do identifiability analysis.
assess_identifiability(osys; measured_quantities=[eq])
```

am trying to write an extension for [Catalyst](https://github.com/SciML/Catalyst.jl)/StructuralIdentifiability that would allow user to input by Catalyst defined `ReactionSystem`s to `assess_identifiability` and `assess_local_identifiability`. 

Basically, `ReactionSystem`s can be converted to `ODESystems`, so I would simply use this conversion. However, generally, Catalyst does not have observables directly defined, so I wanted the user to input these using measured_quantities. Typically, they would just be species of the system, so the user would do something like
```julia StructuralIdentifiability
rs = @reaction_network begin
    (p,d), 0 <--> X
    (k1,k2), X <--> Y
end
assess_identifiability(rs; measured_quantities=[:Y])
```
and the appropriate conversion would be done under the hood. Also, we could do some automatic removal of conserved quantities to e.g. deal with cases like: https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/189

However, I was trying to prototype things following something like: https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/133 but fails. Here is my minimal example:
```julia
# Fectch packages.
using Catalyst, StructuralIdentifiability

# Create model and convert to ODESystem.
rn = @reaction_network begin
    (k1,k2), X <--> Y
end
osys = convert(ODESystem,rn)

# Create equation for observables (X only).
@variables t y(t)
eq = y~states(osys)[1]

# Do identifiability analysis.
assess_identifiability(osys; measured_quantities=[eq])
```
which gives

am trying to write an extension for [Catalyst](https://github.com/SciML/Catalyst.jl)/StructuralIdentifiability that would allow user to input by Catalyst defined `ReactionSystem`s to `assess_identifiability` and `assess_local_identifiability`. 

Basically, `ReactionSystem`s can be converted to `ODESystems`, so I would simply use this conversion. However, generally, Catalyst does not have observables directly defined, so I wanted the user to input these using measured_quantities. Typically, they would just be species of the system, so the user would do something like
```julia StructuralIdentifiability
rs = @reaction_network begin
    (p,d), 0 <--> X
    (k1,k2), X <--> Y
end
assess_identifiability(rs; measured_quantities=[:Y])
```
and the appropriate conversion would be done under the hood. Also, we could do some automatic removal of conserved quantities to e.g. deal with cases like: https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/189

However, I was trying to prototype things following something like: https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/133 but fails. Here is my minimal example:
```julia
# Fectch packages.
using Catalyst, StructuralIdentifiability

# Create model and convert to ODESystem.
rn = @reaction_network begin
    (k1,k2), X <--> Y
end
osys = convert(ODESystem,rn)

# Create equation for observables (X only).
@variables t y(t)
eq = y~states(osys)[1]

# Do identifiability analysis.
assess_identifiability(osys; measured_quantities=[eq])
```

suggest we use this PR to keep track of the progress on implementing the search for identifiable functions of parameters. @pogudingleb @ChrisRackauckas 

As a general outline, three pieces of code are needed: the GB backend in [Groebner.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl), the evaluation-interpolation scheme in [ParamPunPam.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/ParamPunPam.jl), and the frontend in [StructuralIdentifiability.jl](https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl). StructuralIdentifiability.jl will use ParamPunPam.jl to compute Groebner bases with parameters, which, in turn, will use Groebner.jl as a building block. Both ParamPunPam.jl and Groebner.jl are already usable for this purpose to some extent. 

I think a good goal for the very nearest future is to have a working prototype in SI.jl and to look at the challenging examples more closely to decide what needs to be optimized. I suggest the first milestones:

- [ ] Implementing a simple interface in StructuralIdentifiability.jl #187
- [x] Finishing the prep work in Groebner.jl, https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl/pull/67
- [x] Implementing tracing in Groebner.jl

Changelog

v4.0.0

  • Support fetching without the --progress option
  • Update to node20

v3.6.0

  • Fix: Mark test scripts with Bash'isms to be run via Bash
  • Add option to fetch tags even if fetch-depth > 0

v3.5.3

  • Fix: Checkout fail in self-hosted runners when faulty submodule are checked-in
  • Fix typos found by codespell
  • Add support for sparse checkouts

v3.5.2

  • Fix api endpoint for GHES

v3.5.1

  • Fix slow checkout on Windows

v3.5.0

  • Add new public key for known_hosts

v3.4.0

  • Upgrade codeql actions to v2
  • Upgrade dependencies
  • Upgrade @​actions/io

v3.3.0

  • Implement branch list using callbacks from exec function
  • Add in explicit reference to private checkout options
  • [Fix comment typos (that got added in #770)](actions/checkout#1057)

v3.2.0

  • Add GitHub Action to perform release
  • Fix status badge
  • Replace datadog/squid with ubuntu/squid Docker image
  • Wrap pipeline commands for submoduleForeach in quotes
  • Update @​actions/io to 1.1.2
  • Upgrading version to 3.2.0

v3.1.0

  • Use @​actions/core saveState and getState
  • Add github-server-url input

v3.0.2

  • Add input set-safe-directory

suggest we use this PR to keep track of the progress on implementing the search for identifiable functions of parameters. @pogudingleb @ChrisRackauckas 

As a general outline, three pieces of code are needed: the GB backend in [Groebner.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl), the evaluation-interpolation scheme in [ParamPunPam.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/ParamPunPam.jl), and the frontend in [StructuralIdentifiability.jl](https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl). StructuralIdentifiability.jl will use ParamPunPam.jl to compute Groebner bases with parameters, which, in turn, will use Groebner.jl as a building block. Both ParamPunPam.jl and Groebner.jl are already usable for this purpose to some extent. 

I think a good goal for the very nearest future is to have a working prototype in SI.jl and to look at the challenging examples more closely to decide what needs to be optimized. I suggest the first milestones:

- [ ] Implementing a simple interface in StructuralIdentifiability.jl #187
- [x] Finishing the prep work in Groebner.jl, https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl/pull/67
- [x] Implementing tracing in Groebner.jl

-- Alex","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/comments/1709712454","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/pull/185#issuecomment-1709712454","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185","id":1709712454,"node_id":"IC_kwDOEU_GA85l6CRG","user":{"login":"sumiya11","id":60229118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMjI5MTE4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60229118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11","html_url":"https://github.com/sumiya11","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-09-07T08:35:57Z","updated_at":"2023-09-07T08:35:57Z","author_association":"COLLABORATOR","body":"## Benchmark results

suggest we use this PR to keep track of the progress on implementing the search for identifiable functions of parameters. @pogudingleb @ChrisRackauckas 

As a general outline, three pieces of code are needed: the GB backend in [Groebner.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl), the evaluation-interpolation scheme in [ParamPunPam.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/ParamPunPam.jl), and the frontend in [StructuralIdentifiability.jl](https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl). StructuralIdentifiability.jl will use ParamPunPam.jl to compute Groebner bases with parameters, which, in turn, will use Groebner.jl as a building block. Both ParamPunPam.jl and Groebner.jl are already usable for this purpose to some extent. 

I think a good goal for the very nearest future is to have a working prototype in SI.jl and to look at the challenging examples more closely to decide what needs to be optimized. I suggest the first milestones:

- [ ] Implementing a simple interface in StructuralIdentifiability.jl #187
- [x] Finishing the prep work in Groebner.jl, https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl/pull/67
- [x] Implementing tracing in Groebner.jl

As a general outline, three pieces of code are needed: the GB backend in [Groebner.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl), the evaluation-interpolation scheme in [ParamPunPam.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/ParamPunPam.jl), and the frontend in [StructuralIdentifiability.jl](https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl). As a general outline, three pieces of code are needed: the GB backend in [Groebner.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl), the evaluation-interpolation scheme in [ParamPunPam.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/ParamPunPam.jl), and the frontend in [StructuralIdentifiability.jl](https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl). StructuralIdentifiability.jl will use ParamPunPam.jl to compute Groebner bases with parameters, which, in turn, will use Groebner.jl as a building block. Both ParamPunPam.jl and Groebner.jl are already usable. 

I think a good goal for the very nearest future is to have a working prototype in SI.jl and to look at the challenging examples more closely to decide what needs to be optimized. I suggest the first milestones:

- [ ] Implementing a simple interface in StructuralIdentifiability.jl,
- [ ] Finishing the prep work in Groebner.jl, https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl/pull/67.

-- {"action":"opened","number":187,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/pulls/187","id":1416955315,"node_id":"PR_kwDOEU_GA85UdQWz","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/pull/187","diff_url":"https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/pull/187.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/pull/187.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/187","number":187,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"The interface for computing identifiable funcs.","user":{"login":"sumiya11","id":60229118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMjI5MTE4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60229118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11","html_url":"https://github.com/sumiya11","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Basically, we have the function `find_identifiable_functions` that receives a model in the input and gives a list of functions in the output. For example,

```julia
using StructuralIdentifiability
ode = @ODEmodel(
  x1'(t) = -(a01 + a21) * x1(t) + a12 * x2(t) + u(t),
  x2'(t) = a21 * x1(t) - a12 * x2(t) - x3(t) / b,
  x3'(t) = x3(t),
  y(t) = x2(t)
)
find_identifiable_functions(ode)
```

returns

```julia
4-element Vector{AbstractAlgebra.Generic.Frac{Nemo.fmpq_mpoly}}:
 a21
 a12 + a01
 a12*a01 - a12 - a01 - a21
 a12*a01
```

With `simplify=true`, the simplification routine, which uses Groebner bases, is called, and that is the default option.
We should probably consider providing an rng seed in the keyword arguments to be used in Groebner basis suggest we use this PR to keep track of the progress on implementing the search for identifiable functions of parameters. @pogudingleb @ChrisRackauckas As a general outline, three pieces of code are needed: the GB backend in [Groebner.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl), the evaluation-interpolation scheme in [ParamPunPam.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/ParamPunPam.jl), and the frontend in [StructuralIdentifiability.jl](https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl). StructuralIdentifiability.jl will use ParamPunPam.jl to compute Groebner bases with parameters, which, in turn, will use Groebner.jl as a building block. Both ParamPunPam.jl and Groebner.jl are already usable for this purpose to some extent. I think a good goal for the very nearest future is to have a working prototype in SI.jl and to look at the challenging examples more closely to decide what needs to be optimized. I suggest the first milestones: - [ ] Implementing a simple interface in StructuralIdentifiability.jl #187 , - [ ] Finishing the prep work in Groebner.jl, https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl/pull/67. -- suggest we use this PR to keep track of the progress on implementing the search for identifiable functions of parameters. @pogudingleb @ChrisRackauckas 

As a general outline, three pieces of code are needed: the GB backend in [Groebner.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl), the evaluation-interpolation scheme in [ParamPunPam.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/ParamPunPam.jl), and the frontend in [StructuralIdentifiability.jl](https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl). StructuralIdentifiability.jl will use ParamPunPam.jl to compute Groebner bases with parameters, which, in turn, will use Groebner.jl as a building block. Both ParamPunPam.jl and Groebner.jl are already usable for this purpose to some extent. 

I think a good goal for the very nearest future is to have a working prototype in SI.jl and to look at the challenging examples more closely to decide what needs to be optimized. I suggest the first milestones:

- [ ] Implementing a simple interface in StructuralIdentifiability.jl #187 ,

suggest we use this PR to keep track of the progress on implementing the search for identifiable functions of parameters. @pogudingleb @ChrisRackauckas 

As a general outline, three pieces of code are needed: the GB backend in [Groebner.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl), the evaluation-interpolation scheme in [ParamPunPam.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/ParamPunPam.jl), and the frontend in [StructuralIdentifiability.jl](https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl). StructuralIdentifiability.jl will use ParamPunPam.jl to compute Groebner bases with parameters, which, in turn, will use Groebner.jl as a building block. Both ParamPunPam.jl and Groebner.jl are already usable for this purpose to some extent. 

I think a good goal for the very nearest future is to have a working prototype in SI.jl and to look at the challenging examples more closely to decide what needs to be optimized. I suggest the first milestones:

- [ ] Implementing a simple interface in StructuralIdentifiability.jl #187 ,
- [ ] Finishing the prep work in Groebner.jl, https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl/pull/67.

-- Alex","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/comments/1620925311","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/pull/185#issuecomment-1620925311","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185","id":1620925311,"node_id":"IC_kwDOEU_GA85gnVt_","user":{"login":"sumiya11","id":60229118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMjI5MTE4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60229118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11","html_url":"https://github.com/sumiya11","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-07-05T02:21:29Z","updated_at":"2023-07-05T02:21:29Z","author_association":"COLLABORATOR","body":"### A bird's eye view 

...of what `find_identifiable_functions(ode)` currently does, and how it corresponds to the entries in the table.

1. It computes the IO equations of `ode` (the `IO` column in the table).
2. Let $F_1/Q, \ldots, F_n/Q$ be the coefficients of IO eqs. These coefficients already give us all of the identifiable functions! However, they are just too large and there are too many of them, so it is not useful.
3. To find a simpler basis, we construct the ideal $I = \langle Q(x)F_i(y) - Q(y)F_i(x) \rangle : Q(y)^\infty$ in $\mathbb{Q}(x)(y)$ (the `gen. ideal` column). A nice property of $I$ is that the coefficients of the Groebner basis of $I$ give an equivalent and a concise representation of the sought functions.
4. So, we compute the Groebner basis of $I$ (the `ParamPunPam.jl` column), and we are done.

Finding this basis is a challenge -- the elements of $I$ are polynomials in $\mathbb{Q}(x)(y)$, where $x$'s are treated as parameters, and dealing with parametric polynomials is not easy. Here is what we do in `ParamPunPam.jl`.

1. First of all, notice that the basis itself is usually small and simple. To see for yourself, check out the tab **Individual results** in my previous post with benchmarks. There, the first example is SIWR with extra output, and it logs:
- Input: `┌ Info: Given 141 functions...`,
- Output: `┌ Info: The shape of the basis is: 8 polynomials with monomials
│ state.shape = Vector{Nemo.gfp_mpoly}[[y7, 1], [y6, 1], [y5, 1], [y4, 1], [y3, 1], [y2, 1], [y1, 1], [t, 1]]`

That's 141 polynomials in the input and only 8 in the output basis! Even more, the monomials in the basis are just linear in $y$.

The coefficients of the basis, *which is what we want to compute*, are also not large. They mosly have degrees 0 and 1 in $x$:

`┌ Info: The total degrees in the coefficients
│ state.param_degrees = [[(0, 0), (1, 0)], [(0, 0), (1, 0)], [(0, 0), (1, 0)], [(0, 0), (1, 0)], [(0, 0), (1, 0)], [(0, 0), (1, 0)], [(0, 0), (1, 0)], [(0, 0), (0, 12)]]`

2. From here comes a thought: if the output is simple, interpolate it. More precisely:
- Plug in different numerical values for $x$, say, $x_1,\ldots,x_k$. We end up with is a sequence of ideals in $\mathbb{Q}(y)$:

$$
\begin{align*}
I(x_1)(y) = \langle Q(x_1)F_i(y) &- Q(y)F_i(x_1) \rangle : Q(y)^\infty\\
&\ldots\\
I(x_k)(y) = \langle Q(x_k)F_i(y) &- Q(y)F_i(x_k) \rangle : Q(y)^\infty
\end{align*}
$$ 

- For each of $I(x_1)(y), \ldots, I(x_k)(y)$, compute a Groebner basis, $k$ times in total. Let's denote the obtained Groebner bases by $GB(x_1)(y), \ldots, GB(x_k)(y)$. Note that finding $GB(x_i)(y)$ is realistic, since there are no parameters anymore.

- Interpolate the coefficients of the true $GB(x)(y)$ from their evaluations $GB(x_1)(y), \ldots, GB(x_k)(y)$ using some exact interpolation algorithm to avoud approximation errors.

The principal idea is that since the coefficients in $x$ are simple, the required $k$ is small. In the second table, `# points` stands for $k$, and `main loop` stands for evaluation-interpolation runtime.

Once the basis is interpolated, we are done. We can then check that the result is correct with a high probability","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/comments/1620925311/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

-- Alex","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/comments/1620927906","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/pull/185#issuecomment-1620927906","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185","id":1620927906,"node_id":"IC_kwDOEU_GA85gnWWi","user":{"login":"sumiya11","id":60229118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMjI5MTE4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60229118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11","html_url":"https://github.com/sumiya11","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-07-05T02:27:04Z","updated_at":"2023-07-05T02:27:04Z","author_association":"COLLABORATOR","body":"So, computing GBs takes most of the time.
For benchmark systems that did not finish, GB usually was a bottleneck.

Here us a list of things that we can do next, ordered from more specific to less. Let me know if you have some other ideas!

- **Compute GBs degree by degree**. Notice, e.g., in the SIWR with extra output example from my previous post, there are many coefficients of degree 1, and one coefficients of degree 12. Interpolating the latter one is useless, as it can be expressed in terms of the smaller coefficients. In this example, we can interpolate the coefficients up to degree 1, which is much faster than going up to degree 12, check that the result is correct, and be done.
- **Better preprocess input**. As an example, check out the Pharm model in the tab Individual results. It has over 3.5k input polynomials for GB. Out of those, 200 or so are duplicates up to a multiplication by a constant. Potentially, even more can be eliminated in the very beginning with a gaussian elimination pass. Another question here: can we somehow simplify the input polynomials $Q(x_k)F_i(y) - Q(y)F_i(x_k)$ ?
- **Check for unlucky evaluations**. We have a couple of examples in the table that I highly suspect to be an unlucky prime or evaluation point. Verify that this is what happens, and check for such errors more rigorously.
- **Use tracing.** Currently, we hardly use any information about the already computed $GB(x_{1})(y)$ to compute $GB(x_{i})(y)$. The proposition is to implement tracing that would speed up the evaluations for large $k$ by using the previous computation as a boilerplate (it probably comes with a small slowdown of computing the very first $GB(x_{1})(y)$). 
- Currently, we add globablly identifiable parameters to the initial generators of the ideal to speed up GB. Try also adding pairwise combinations of those, and so on (and add a check -- don't compute GB if all parameters are globally id., just return them).
- When the number of variables or the degree is large (10 variables of degree 20 seems to be the threshold), there is a risk that the interpolation will fail. As a solution, we can use more sophisitcated interpolation algorithms which try to handle such cases, but I am not enthusiastic about implementing those. Another option is to compute over a larger field, up to `2^128` or `2^256` bits instead of `2^64`, which would slow us down by the factor of `4` or `16` respectively. Hmm..
- Think about saturation. Eliminate $t$ from here somehow?
$$I = \langle Q(x)F_i(y) - Q(y)F_i(x), 1 - t Q(y)\rangle$$
- Check out the `slimgb` algorithm for computing Groebner bases over rational functions directly. Also, check out the "graph of computations" method -- I think is has potential.

As a general outline, three pieces of code are needed: the GB backend in [Groebner.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl), the evaluation-interpolation scheme in [ParamPunPam.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/ParamPunPam.jl), and the frontend in [StructuralIdentifiability.jl](https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl). StructuralIdentifiability.jl will use ParamPunPam.jl to compute Groebner bases with parameters, which, in turn, will use Groebner.jl as a building block. Both ParamPunPam.jl and Groebner.jl are already usable for this purpose to some extent. 

I think a good goal for the very nearest future is to have a working prototype in SI.jl and to look at the challenging examples more closely to decide what needs to be optimized. I suggest the first milestones:

- [ ] Implementing a simple interface in StructuralIdentifiability.jl #187
- [x] Finishing the prep work in Groebner.jl, https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl/pull/67
- [x] Implementing tracing in Groebner.jl

-- Alex","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/comments/1634291907","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/pull/185#issuecomment-1634291907","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185","id":1634291907,"node_id":"IC_kwDOEU_GA85haVDD","user":{"login":"sumiya11","id":60229118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMjI5MTE4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60229118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11","html_url":"https://github.com/sumiya11","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-07-13T13:55:12Z","updated_at":"2023-07-13T13:55:12Z","author_association":"COLLABORATOR","body":"## Benchmark results

suggest we use this PR to keep track of the progress on implementing the search for identifiable functions of parameters. @pogudingleb @ChrisRackauckas 

As a general outline, three pieces of code are needed: the GB backend in [Groebner.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl), the evaluation-interpolation scheme in [ParamPunPam.jl](https://github.com/sumiya11/ParamPunPam.jl), and the frontend in [StructuralIdentifiability.jl](https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl). StructuralIdentifiability.jl will use ParamPunPam.jl to compute Groebner bases with parameters, which, in turn, will use Groebner.jl as a building block. Both ParamPunPam.jl and Groebner.jl are already usable for this purpose to some extent. 

I think a good goal for the very nearest future is to have a working prototype in SI.jl and to look at the challenging examples more closely to decide what needs to be optimized. I suggest the first milestones:

- [ ] Implementing a simple interface in StructuralIdentifiability.jl #187
- [x] Finishing the prep work in Groebner.jl, https://github.com/sumiya11/Groebner.jl/pull/67
- [x] Implementing tracing in Groebner.jl

-- Alex","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/comments/1643570826","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/pull/185#issuecomment-1643570826","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/StructuralIdentifiability.jl/issues/185","id":1643570826,"node_id":"IC_kwDOEU_GA85h9uaK","user":{"login":"sumiya11","id":60229118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMjI5MTE4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60229118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11","html_url":"https://github.com/sumiya11","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-07-20T09:16:28Z","updated_at":"2023-07-20T09:16:28Z","author_association":"COLLABORATOR","body":"## Benchmark results

Probably, there is plenty of invalidations and runtime dispatch happening, but I think for now this should be good.

Current:
```julia
 @time using StructuralIdentifiability
  7.602019 seconds (7.80 M allocations: 521.949 MiB, 4.19% gc time, 0.72% compilation time)

@time begin
    ode = @ODEmodel(x1'(t) = a * x1(t), y(t) = x1(t))
    assess_identifiability(ode)
end;
 39.708916 seconds (41.74 M allocations: 2.672 GiB, 4.57% gc time, 99.77% compilation time)
```

This pr:
```julia
@time using StructuralIdentifiability
  9.382278 seconds (8.58 M allocations: 561.076 MiB, 3.45% gc time, 0.67% compilation time)

@time begin
    ode = @ODEmodel(x1'(t) = a * x1(t), y(t) = x1(t))
    assess_identifiability(ode)
end;
0.288237 seconds (77.30 k allocations: 6.393 MiB, 80.49% compilation time)
```

For some reason the logger does not propagate to the `@ODEmodel`, as a result during the precompilation it logs stuff, which is not nice. Any idea how to fix this?
```julia
using StructuralIdentifiability
[ Info: Precompiling StructuralIdentifiability [220ca800-aa68-49bb-acd8-6037fa93a544]
[ Info: Summary of the model:
[ Info: State variables: x2, x3, x1
[ Info: Parameters: b, a12, a01, a21
[ Info: Inputs: u
[ Info: Outputs: y
```

functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2022-12-25T14:56:19Z","updated_at":"2023-03-29T20:09:53Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/47995","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47995","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47995.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47995.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Hello! This PR improves `MultiplicativeInverses` for `Int128` and `UInt128`. PR consists of 3 tiny points:

- Previously, division with multiplicative inverses used `widen`. It widened 128-bit ints to `BigInt`, which hindered performance. The solution in this PR is to replace `widen` with higher half multiplication for `Int128` and `UInt128`.
```julia
# 1.8.2
using BenchmarkTools
a, b = rand(UInt128), rand(UInt128)
d = Base.MultiplicativeInverses.UnsignedMultiplicativeInverse(b)

@btime div($a, $d)
  645.455 ns (12 allocations: 216 bytes)
```

```julia
# this pr
using BenchmarkTools
a, b = rand(UInt128), rand(UInt128)
d = Base.MultiplicativeInverses.UnsignedMultiplicativeInverse(b)

@btime div($a, $d)
  6.800 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

- Added tests for `MultiplicativeInverses` for all bit integer types;
- Draw attention to the fact that signed division with `MultiplicativeInverses` does not throw when the result overflows (see tests)

As for the latter, it's alright with me, but I think it would be good to mention this fact somewhere

Also, I've coded the higher half mult. from scratch, so let me know if there were functions for this in Base already This PR improves `MultiplicativeInverses` for `Int128` and `UInt128`. PR consists of 3 tiny points:

- Previously, division with multiplicative inverses used `widen`. It widened 128-bit ints to `BigInt`, which hindered performance. The solution in this PR is to replace `widen` with higher half multiplication for `Int128` and `UInt128`.
```julia
# 1.8.2
using BenchmarkTools
a, b = rand(UInt128), rand(UInt128)
d = Base.MultiplicativeInverses.UnsignedMultiplicativeInverse(b)

@btime div($a, $d)
  645.455 ns (12 allocations: 216 bytes)
```

```julia
# this pr
using BenchmarkTools
a, b = rand(UInt128), rand(UInt128)
d = Base.MultiplicativeInverses.UnsignedMultiplicativeInverse(b)

@btime div($a, $d)
  6.800 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

- Added tests for `MultiplicativeInverses` for all bit integer types;
- Draw attention to the fact that signed division with `MultiplicativeInverses` does not throw when the result overflows (see tests)

As for the latter, it's alright with me, but I think it would be good to mention this fact somewhere

Also, I've coded the higher half mult. from scratch, so let me know if there were functions for this in Base already 😊","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47995/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47995/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1426706791","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/47995#issuecomment-1426706791","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47995","id":1426706791,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5VCdFn","user":{"login":"sumiya11","id":60229118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMjI5MTE4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60229118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11","html_url":"https://github.com/sumiya11","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sumiya11/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-02-11T11:42:06Z","updated_at":"2023-02-11T11:42:06Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> Also, do you happen to know if there's an efficient way to compute a*b%n for Int128 without widening to BigInt?
